content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

Technical Assistance • • Administration • • Executive/Legal • • Enforcement • • Statements of Economic Interest
(916) 322-5662 322-5660 322-5901 322-6441 322-6444

November 5, 1982

Donald L. Cole
P.O. Box 1047
Red Bluff, CA 96080

Re: Advice Letter No. A-82-186

Dear Mr. Cole:

Thank you for your request for advice regarding the conflict
of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act. The facts,
as you presented them to me, are that you are the newly-elected
coroner of Tehama County. You currently own 1,225 shares of
Dendon Service Corporation, which does business, as Hoyt-Coyle
Chapel of the Flowers and W. C. Pickert Memorial Chapel, in Red
Bluff (Tehama County). The shareholders who operate the
mortaries1/ have a thirty-year option to purchase your
shares. The payments will be from the company, and you will
receive $1,970 per month. You asked whether your interest in
Dendon Corporation creates a conflict of interest for you, in
your position as coroner.

As we discussed on the telephone, the conflict of interest
provisions of the Political Reform Act do not prevent you from
serving as coroner. Rather, the Act may require you to
disqualify yourself from making and participating in certain
governmental decisions.

Government Code Section 871002/ provides that no local
government official may make, or participate in the making of, a

_____________________

1/ Your son and ex-wife are among these shareholders.

2/ Hereinafter all statutory references are to the
Government Code, unless otherwise indicated.

Donald L. Cole
November 5, 1982
Page 2


decision in which he knows or has reason to know he has a
financial interest. An official has a financial interest within
the meaning of Section 87100 if it is reasonably foreseeable
that the decision will have a material financial effect on:

     (a) Any business in which the public official has
a direct or indirect investment worth more than one
thousand dollars ($1,000).

     . . .

     (c) Any source of income . . . aggregating two
hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value
received by . . . the public official within 12 months
prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section
87103(a) and (c).)

     The stock interest you own in Dendon Corporation is an
investment interest worth more than $1,000.2/  Also, the
company is a source of income to you of $250 or more because of
its monthly payments to you for your stock. (Section 87103(a)
and (c).) Thus, in your position as coroner, you may not make,
or participate in the making of, or use of your official
position to influence, decisions which will have a reasonably
foreseeable material financial effect on Dendon Corporation and
the mortuaries that it operates.

     A decision will have a material financial effect on the
company if the effect of the decision will be to increase or
decrease:

     (A) The annualized gross revenues by the lessor
of:

          1. $100,000; or

          2. 1% if the effect is one thousand dollars
or more; or

_________________
3/  If any ownership interest in Dendon Corporation
is held by a dependent child of yours, this interest is
considered to be held by you personally. (Section 8203d.)

     At some point, the shares you own in the company may
decline in number so that the remaining shares are no
longer worth $1,000.

Donald L. Cole
November 5, 1982
Page 3


(B)  Annual net income by the lesser of;

     1.  $50,000; or

     2.  1/2% if the effect is $1,000 or more; or

(C)  Current assets or liabilities by the lesser
of:

     1.  $100,000; or

     2.  1/2% if the effect is $1,000 or more.
(2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702(b)(1)(A)-(C).)

     In your letter, you specifically asked whether the existing
method for assigning "coroner's cases" to mortuaries presented a
conflict for you under this system, if a family does not
choose a moruary for a coroner's case, the body is assigned to
one of the three mortuaries in the County, based upon whether
the death occured in the North or South part of the County.
The mortuaries owned by Dendon Corporation are both in Red Bluff
and they share the "North area" assignments. Your concern is
that there is a conflict in your approving these assignments.

     The phrase "making or participating in the making of a
governmental decision" does not include actions of a public
official which are solely ministerial. (2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18700(d)(1). Under the system you described, the
assignment of cases appears to be predetermined and the coroner
does not seem to exercise any discretion in approving the
assignments. If this is the case, you may continue to use the
existing system. However, if you are confronted with a decision
as to whether to continue, alter, or alter, the system used for
assigning bodies, you probably will be required to disqualify
yourself, as it is likely that this decision will have a
material financial effect on Dendon Corporation.

     On the telephone you stated that the County is considering
contracting with Dendon Corporation to have it store bodies for
the County in its newly-installed refrigerators, rather than
ship the bodies to Reading, as is currently done.4/  This
decision may easily have a reasonably foreseeable material
financial effect on the company, requiring you to disqualify

___________________
     4/ You should contact the Attorney General's Office
regarding the possible application of Section 1090.

Donald L. Cole
November 5, 1982
Page 4


yourself from making, influencing, or participating in the
decision. You must apply the facts of the situation to the
tests outlined in 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18702 (see above) to
determine whether you must abstain.

I hope I have answered your questions for you. If I can be
of any further help to you, please feel free to contact me at
(916) 322-5901.

Very truly yours,


Janis Shank McLean
Counsel
Legal Division

JSM:pla

Donald L. Cole
P.O. Box 1047
Red Bluff, CA 96080

OCT 12 8 43 AM 85

Janice McClean, Atty.
F.P.P.C.
Sacramento, CA

Dear Ms. McClean:

Before the primary election you thoughtfully sent to me the booklet on conflicts
of interest. .My opponent (the incumbent) is again claiming that I will have a
conflict and addition to 42 OPS CAL A G 151,is referring to a "mini opinion" by
the A.G.'s office. The attorney general's office assures me that no such opinion
has been issued since 1963.

To refresh your memory, my wife and I,with our son,held a family owned corporation
which operated two mortuaries in Red Bluff (Tehama County). When my wife and I
seperated and decided on divorces, I gave up my interest in the corporation and have
not been associated with the business since early this year. Enclosed is a copy
of the agreement which cuts me loose from funeral service.

You may recall from our earlier discussion that there is, and has been for several
years, an equitable, working plan for distribution of "Coroner's cases" in Tehama
county. In those coroner related deaths where the family does not select a mortuary,
or where there is no family to make a selection, decedents are directed either to
the mortuary in Corning (south county), or to Red Bluff depending on the geographical
place of death. I have asked that a representative of the Corning mortuary serve as
a deputy coroner.

In response to an Opinion Request CV 76/78 IL, the Attorney General concluded that
a conflict existed with the incumbent due to a contract for ambulance service with
the County of Tehama. It is not my contention that such a conflict exists, I am
challenging him on the basis of his inability to perform (a matter of public record)
due to the demands of his private ambulance service & medical equipment business.
He does, however, accept a full salary & benefits.

I can provide better service to the county with an overall savings but I do want
to be legal in every way. I have researched the law library and find nothing to
indicate a conflict if elected. Incidentally, even though the vote was split 3 ways,
I out-drew the incumbent nearly two to one in the primary an missed winning by six
tenths of a percent. Any comments or suggestions you might have would be appreciated.

gratefully yours,

Donald L. Cole
candidate for Coroner & P.A.
Tehama County

Enc(1)

OPTION FOR REDEMPTION OF SHARES OF STOCK


THIS AGREEMENT IS MADE effective this ____ day of

July, 1982, at Red Bluff, Tehama County, California, between

DENDON SERVICE CORPORATION, a California corporation, doing

business as HOYT-COLE CHAPEL OF THE FLOWERS and W. C.

FICKERT MEMORIAL CHAPEL, hereinafter referred to as "Company,"

DONALD L. COLE, hereinafter referred to as "Selling Share-

holder," VIRGINIA A. COLE, DENNIS L. COLE, and SANDRA L.

COLE, hereinafter referred to as "Purchasing Shareholders":

SHARES

1. Selling Shareholder is the owner of 1,225 shares

of issued and outstanding capital stock of Company, which

shares are the subject matter of this agreement, and are

hereinafter referred to as "said shares."

2. In and for valuable consideration, receipt of

which is hereby acknowledged, Selling Shareholder gives to

the Company or Purchasing Shareholders the right, title,

and option to redeem and/or purchase all of Selling Share-

holder's shares in said corporation, said option to be for

a period of thirty (30) years from and after the date

hereof.

3. Whether the shares of stock shall be redeemed by

the Company or shall be purchased outright by the Purchasing

Shareholders or shall be divided between the Purchasing

Shareholders and the company, or divided between the Purchas-

ing Shareholders themselves, shall be something solely for

the decision of the Purchasing Shareholders and the Company.

4. The purchase price for said shares, either for the

redemption, cancelation, and/or reissuance shall be the

-1-

total principal sum of $224,212, together with interest on
the decreasing balance of said principal sum at the rate of
ten (10) percent per annum, interest to commence July ____,
1982. Said principal and interest shall be payable in
installments of $1,970.00 or more each month, with the
first such installment being due and payable August 15,
1982, and a like installment due on the 15th day of each
and every month thereafter until said principal and interest
are paid in full.

     5. Commencing with the 15th day of August, 1983,
there shall be transferred over to the Company or the
Purchasing Shareholders, as many shares as have been paid
for at the rate of $184.00 per share (it being understood
that the actual breakdown of the purchase price per share
would be the sum of $183.0302). At this time there shall
be deemed to be exercised the option to purchase the shares,
and the number of shares then paid for shall be transferred
over to the Company or the Purchasing Shareholders pursuant
to the terms hereof. Said option shall thereafter be
exercised automatically each August 15th throughout the
term of this agreement unless cancelled by Company and
Purchasing Shareholders or upon a failure to make payments.

     6. Upon the exercise of the option, the Company
acknowledges its primary and unconditional obligation to
redeem the shares described in this agreement. However, in
the event that it is unable to redeem "said shares" because
of limitations described in Sections 500 of the California
Corporations Code, Purchasing Shareholders agree to be
secondarily liable to purchase from Selling Shareholder
only those shares that cannot legally be redeemed pursuant

-2-

to Sections 500, et seq., of the California Corporations

Code.  These obligations to purchase the "said shares"

shall, of course, only arise upon the exercise of the

option to purchase the shares.

     7.  In connection with the sale by him to Company or

Purchasing Shareholders of his shares of stock, Selling

Shareholder agrees that he shall not, either as an owner,

part owner, manager, operator, employee, salesman, agent,

financier, or other participant, engaged in the morturary

business for a period of thirty (30) years after the execution

of this agreement, within the County of Tehama.  This

covenant shall not, however, prohibit Selling Shareholder

from carrying out the duties and activities of the Office

of Coroner of the County of Tehama.

     8.  Selling Shareholder warrants that he is the owner

of the shares subject to the terms and conditions of this

agreement, that "said shares" are not in any way encumbered,

and that he has the right to transfer "said shares" pursuant

to the terms of this agreement, and subject only to the

legend condition imposed upon "said shares" by the law of

the State of California.

     9.  In order to effectuate the operation of the Company

by the Purchasing Shareholders, the Selling Shareholder

shall execute in favor of the Purchasing Shareholders an

irrevocable proxy for the option period under the terms of

this agreement, to wit:  thirty (30) years, a copy of said

proxy being attached hereto, marked Exhibit "A" and in-

corporated herein by this reference.

     10.  Contemporaneously with the execution of this

option agreement, Selling Shareholder shall resign from the

                                    -3-

Board of Directors of the corporation, and shall resign

from his corporate office with the corporation.  Said

resignation shall only be effective as long as this option

is in force and effect, and the payments are being made as

heretofore provided.  Should this option terminate, the

resignation shall no longer be deemed effective, and the

proxy to be executed in favor of the Purchasing Shareholders

as set forth in paragraph 9 shall be deemed revoked, with

the Selling Shareholder being given full right, title, and

privileges as a shareholder of the Company, including but

not limited to the right to call shareholders' meetings for

the election of new officers and board members or for any

other purpose.

     11.  Purchasing Shareholders agree to hold Selling

Shareholder free and harmless from any and all obligations

created on behalf of the Company during the option period,

which are not created in the normal and ordinary course of

business and operation of the Company's business.  This

hold harmless provision shall survive and supercede the

option whether or not the same is exercised and/or terminated.

     12.  Except as has occurred in the normal course of

business, none of the Company's assets are to be pledged,

encumbered or hypothecated during the period of this option

without the expressed written consent of the Selling Share-

holder and any such pledge, hypothecation or encumbrance

placed against the Company assets during the period of this

option shall be the sole responsibility of the Purchasing

Shareholders, and the Purchasing Shareholders shall hold

the Selling Shareholder totally free and harmless from any

and all obligations thereunder.  None of the Company's

-4-

assets shall be transferred out of the Company, except in
the ordinary course of business, during this option period,
except with the written consent of the Selling Shareholder.
The Selling Shareholder shall not, however, withhold his
written consent to any such pledge, hypothecation, encumbrance
or conveyance of corporate assets if the same are being
done in the best interests of the Company's business.

Except for the election of new officers and members of the
board of directors to fulfill the place of the Selling
Shareholder, the Purchasing Shareholders shall not allow
the corporate structure to be changed in any way during the
option period, including, but not limited to, the election
to issue any more shares of stock in the corporation which
will in any way deplete the percentage interest in the
corporation that the Selling Shareholder now has. Nothing
herein, however, shall prevent the transfer of now issued
shares of stock from and between the Purchasing Shareholders
themselves in any amount or number.

13. This option constitutes the sole and only agreement
between Company, Selling Shareholder, and Purchasing Share-
holders respecting the said shares described herein, and
the redemption and/or purchase described in this agreement,
and correctly sets for the obligation of Company, Selling
Shareholder, and Purchasing Shareholders to each other as
of its date. Any agreements or representations other than
those specifically set forth herein are null and void.

14. Should any litigation be commenced between the
parties to this agreement concerning said business, "said
shares," this agreement, or the rights and duties of the
parties hereto, in relation thereto, the parties, Company,

-5-

Selling Shareholder, or Purchasing Shareholders prevailing
in such litigation shall be entitled, in addition to such
other relief as may be granted, to a reasonable sum as and
for their attorneys' fees in such litigation, which shall
be determined in such litigation or in separate action
brought for that purpose.

IN WITNESS WHEREOF, the parties hereto have hereunto
set their hands the day and year first hereinabove written.

DENDON SERVICE CORPORATION,
a California corporation

By_______________________________
ITS PRESIDENT

By_______________________________
ITS SECRETARY

COMPANY

_______________________________
VIRGINIA A. COLE

_______________________________
DENNIS L. COLE

_______________________________
SANDRA L. COLE

PURCHASING
SHAREHOLDERS

_______________________________
DONALD L. COLE

SELLING
SHAREHOLDER




-6-

STOCK PROXY

THIS PROXY is made effective this ____ day of July,

1982, between DENNIS L. COLE, SANDRA L. COLE, and VIRGINIA

A. COLE, hereinafter referred to as "Purchasers," and

DONALD L. COLE, hereinafter referred to as "Seller":

1. In connection with an option of even date herewith,

the Seller hereby grants to the Purchasers his proxy and

grants to the Purchasers the right to vote all Seller's shares

of stock in DENDON SERVICE CORPORATION, a California corporation,

in the place and stead of the Seller, subject to the limitations

as set forth in said option agreement.

2. During the term of this proxy, all dividends or

other distributions made by the company on account of his

capital stock, if any, shall paid to Purchasers, with

Purchasers being obligated to or pay to Seller any such

dividends or other distributions as to any stock not purchase

by them pursuant to the terms of option agreement.

3. If any of the provisions of this proxy or the

provisions of the option executed in connection therewith

are violated by the Purchasers, then this proxy shall

immediately cease and no longer be in force and effect.

_____________________________
DONALD L. COLE

Purchasing Shareholders hereby acknowledge that they

have read, approved, and accept the terms of the foregoing

proxy and agree to be bound thereby.

_____________________________
DENNIS L. COLE

_____________________________
SANDRA L. COLE

_____________________________
VIRGINIA A. COLE

sections.question: You asked whether your interest in Dendon Corporation creates a conflict of interest for you, in your position as coroner. In your letter, you specifically asked whether the existing method for assigning "coroner's cases" to mortuaries presented a conflict for you.
sections.conclusion: The conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act do not prevent you from serving as coroner. Rather, the Act may require you to disqualify yourself from making and participating in certain governmental decisions. You may not make, or participate in the making of, or use your official position to influence, decisions which will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on Dendon Corporation and the mortuaries that it operates. Under the current system where case assignments appear predetermined with no discretion by the coroner, you may continue to use the existing system. However, you would be required to disqualify yourself if confronted with decisions to continue or alter the assignment system, or regarding the proposed body storage contract.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether a newly-elected coroner's ownership of 1,225 shares in Dendon Service Corporation (which operates two mortuaries in the county that receive case assignments) creates a conflict of interest under the Political Reform Act, and whether the current ministerial system for assigning coroner's cases to mortuaries presents a conflict.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC advises that while coroner service is permitted, Mr. Cole must disqualify himself from decisions that would have a material financial effect on Dendon Corporation, including decisions to modify the case assignment system or to contract with the corporation for body storage services. Ministerial case assignments under the current system do not create a conflict if no discretion is exercised.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains the primary FPPC advice letter dated November 5, 1982, plus a follow-up correspondence from the applicant and a stock option agreement. The question and conclusion sections were clearly identified in the body of the advice letter. High confidence in extraction as the document structure is clear despite initial note that no section headers were found.

QUESTION: You asked whether your interest in Dendon Corporation creates a conflict of interest for you, in your position as coroner. In your letter, you specifically asked whether the existing method for assigning "coroner's cases" to mortuaries presented a conflict for you.

CONCLUSION: The conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act do not prevent you from serving as coroner. Rather, the Act may require you to disqualify yourself from making and participating in certain governmental decisions. You may not make, or participate in the making of, or use your official position to influence, decisions which will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on Dendon Corporation and the mortuaries that it operates. Under the current system where case assignments appear predetermined with no discretion by the coroner, you may continue to use the existing system. However, you would be required to disqualify yourself if confronted with decisions to continue or alter the assignment system, or regarding the proposed body storage contract.

The shareholders who operate the mortaries1/ have a thirty-year option to purchase your shares. The payments will be from the company, and you will receive $1,970 per month. As we discussed on the telephone, the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act do not prevent you from serving as coroner. Rather, the Act may require you to disqualify yourself from making and participating in certain governmental decisions. (Section 87103(a) and (c).) (Section 87103(a) and (c).) Thus, in your position as coroner, you may not make, or participate in the making of, or use of your official position to influence, decisions which will have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on Dendon Corporation and the mortuaries that it operates. embedding.summary: This advice letter addresses whether a newly-elected coroner's shareholding in a funeral services corporation and receipt of $1,970 monthly payments creates a conflict of interest, concluding that while service is permitted, disqualification is required for certain decisions affecting the corporation. The document includes a follow-up letter from the applicant clarifying that he has since divested his interest in the corporation and the stock option agreement memorializing the financial arrangement.
